summary: I was living in Chicago when I entered the service in April 1944. I grew up on the Northside, around Milwaukee, Armitage, and Western, at 2440 West Courtland Street. I went to Lane Tech High School there and had a good life playing sports. Chicago was home to me and my family before I was drafted into the Army.

I attended Lane Tech High School in Chicago and graduated in January 1944. I loved it there, especially playing basketball, football, and baseball. The teachers and athletic coaches were wonderful, and it was a big part of my youth before joining the Army. Many of my friends from Lane Tech went to Europe during the war.

My brother Walter worked on the lenses for the Norden Bombsight during the war, which was considered important military work. Because of that, he was deferred from joining the Army. I remember that his work on the bombsight lenses was critical and kept him from being drafted.

My parents came from Poland, and it was very personal for me during the war. My Uncle Andrew was killed by the Nazis on his farm there, which deeply affected my family. My mother cried over the loss, and that was part of why I wanted to join the Army and fight in Europe.

Nazi Germany was the enemy that took my uncle’s life in Poland. I wanted to join the Army early to fight against them in Europe, but I ended up being sent to the Pacific instead. The Nazis and their SS were responsible for many atrocities that affected my family personally.

I was drafted into the United States Army in April 1944 after finishing high school. I trained at Fort Sheridan and Fort Riley before being sent overseas. The Army gave me food, clothing, and tough training, including working with horses and mules in the 124th Cavalry Regiment. I served in the Pacific Theater during the war.

My brother Stanley joined the Coast Guard during the war. While I was drafted into the Army, he served in the Coast Guard, so we were both contributing to the war effort in different branches. It was a family effort to support the country.

I didn’t serve in the United States Marine Corps, but when I was drafted, I didn’t have a preference for any branch. I was willing to go wherever they needed me, whether it was the Marines, Coast Guard, Army Air Corps, or Army. Ultimately, I was placed in the Army.

Like with the Marines and Coast Guard, I was open to serving in the Army Air Corps if needed. However, I ended up in the Cavalry Regiment in the Army. I didn’t get to choose my assignment, as they sent me where they needed me most during the war.

After being inducted at the post office in Chicago, I was sent to Fort Sheridan for initial processing. It was my first time away from home, and it was a big adjustment. From there, I went on to Fort Riley for further training.

Fort Riley, Kansas was where I went after Fort Sheridan for basic training with the 124th Cavalry Regiment. It was tough training with forced marches, rifle shooting, and working with horses and mules. I spent a lot of time there preparing for deployment overseas.

Sergeant Porter, our tough drill sergeant at Fort Riley, was from Texas. He was very strict and kept us moving all the time during training. Texas was part of the diverse backgrounds of the men I served with in the Army.

I met soldiers from all over the country during training, including guys from New York. One of my friends, Dick Miller, was from Salt Lake City, but we had others from places like New York and Kentucky. It was a real mix of backgrounds in the Army.

One of my close friends in the Army was from Kentucky, a man we called Clay. He came from a very poor background with a dirt floor house and no running water. Despite our different upbringings, we became very close friends during the war.

After finishing training at Fort Riley, we were sent to California, specifically Riverside, for new equipment and preparation before shipping out. California was the last stop in the U.S. before we boarded the ship to go overseas. It was a clue we were headed to the Pacific.

My friend Dick Miller was from Salt Lake City, Utah. We had some friendly religious teasing since I was Catholic and he was a Mormon, but we became best friends. He still lives in Utah and has a home in Beaver Dam, Arizona where I visited him.

Utah was home to my good friend Dick Miller, who was a Mormon. We joked about the tough times Mormons had in Illinois, but we stayed close friends through the war and beyond. He retired and lives partly in Utah and partly in Arizona.
I visited my friend Dick Miller at his home in Beaver Dam, Arizona. It’s a beautiful place, and he and his wife were very welcoming. It was nice to see him after all these years since the war.

I was born during the Great Depression, and I remember how hard times were. My mother had to put cardboard in my shoes because the leather wore out. People in our neighborhood lost jobs and homes, and some even committed suicide. It was a tough childhood before the war.

I served in the 124th Cavalry Regiment during the war. Although I didn’t know much about horses or mules before, I learned quickly and loved working with them. We used mules to carry equipment, and they even shipped the animals overseas with us.

Major Stadler wrote a history of our unit, the 124th Cavalry Regiment, which I brought to the interview. My memory isn’t perfect, but his report helped fill in details about our service. He was an important figure documenting our experiences.

The Merchant Marine was responsible for transporting the horses and mules overseas separately from the troops. While we were on one ship, the animals came on another. They played a key role in moving supplies and equipment during the war.

Our ship stopped in Melbourne, Australia for a break during the long voyage to the Pacific. We got to go ashore, rent bicycles, and even met some local girls who were very kind to us. It was a pleasant and memorable stop before continuing on to India.

We traveled through Australia on our way to the Pacific Theater. Melbourne was our main stop where we enjoyed some time off the ship. The people were friendly and welcoming, which was a nice contrast to the harsh conditions we later faced.

When we reached Bombay, India, it was a terrible place compared to Australia. The poverty was overwhelming with many people starving and begging. It was so bad that we felt safer on the ship than on shore, and it really depressed me to see such suffering.

Our journey took us through India, stopping in places like Bombay and Calcutta. The poverty there was shocking, especially in Bombay where people were begging and starving. It was a harsh reality that contrasted with my experiences back home and in Australia.

Calcutta was one of the places we passed through in India during the war. I didn’t spend much time there, but it was part of the route we took to reach the combat zones in the Pacific. The conditions in India were tough to witness.

Assam was part of the region in India near where we operated in the China-Burma-India Theater. It was a remote and difficult area, and our unit had to adapt to tough jungle conditions. The terrain and climate were challenging for all of us.

While I was not a member of Merrill's Marauders, they were a well-known unit operating in the same general area of the China-Burma-India Theater. Our unit worked in similar conditions, facing the Japanese in Burma. Their reputation was something we all respected.

The Japanese Empire was the enemy we faced in the Pacific. They captured our food supplies at times, and their submarines threatened our troop transport ships. Fighting them in Burma and the Pacific was difficult and dangerous, and we lost a lot of weight due to scarce food.

I served in Burma during the war, where conditions were harsh and food was scarce. Our unit used mules to carry equipment through the jungle terrain. The Japanese controlled much of the area, and we had to fight hard to survive and complete our missions.
